content.problemStatement:
You have a stack which is initially empty. You receive instructions of the form 1 or 0. 1 denotes that you have to push in an element, and 0 denotes that you have to pop out an element. But of course you can pop something out only if it exists in the stack. So your job is to look at the instructions and see if they are valid (i.e., you never have to pop from an empty stack), or not.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT, which denotes the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer nnn, which denotes the number of instructions.
The second line contains nnn space-separated integers, which are all 0 or 1.


Output Format

For each test case, output either "Valid" or "Invalid", in a new line.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T, which denotes the number of test cases. The first line of each test case contains a single integer n, which denotes the number of instructions. The second line contains n space-separated integers, which are all 0 or 1.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output either "Valid" or "Invalid", in a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Testcase 2: You first push something, and then pop it out. So now you have an empty stack, but the next operation is a Pop, and hence this is Invalid.
Explanation: You push two elements and then pop one, so the stack is not empty and the operations are valid.
Explanation: You try to pop an element from an empty stack, so the operations are invalid.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can simulate the stack operations. We start with an empty stack and iterate over the instructions. If the instruction is 1, we push an element onto the stack. If the instruction is 0, we check if the stack is empty. If it is, we output "Invalid". Otherwise, we pop an element from the stack. After iterating over all instructions, if we haven't output "Invalid", we output "Valid".
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*n) — where T is the number of test cases and n is the number of instructions in each test case. This is because we iterate over each instruction in each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) — where n is the number of instructions in each test case. This is because in the worst case, we might need to push all instructions onto the stack.
